question_frequency.rationale: The Pair posed two substantive multiple-choice forks (sqrt vs. linear divisor loop, and inline vs. precomputed) plus a follow-up efficiency question. Given the problem's small surface area, this is high-frequency engagement at the meaningful decision points.

question_quality.rationale: Both forks target real trade-offs: the O(√n) vs O(n) divisor algorithm (the central efficiency idea) and caching vs recomputation with the bounds-check gotcha. The questions miss a chance to probe the perfect-number edge case directly, but they hit the core algorithmic cruxes.

scaffolding.rationale: Logical progression from inner function to outer loop, with difficulty escalating appropriately. The options are presented without spoiling — though showing the full diff with the bounds check effectively previews that subtlety rather than letting the student discover it.

flow_and_tone.rationale: Tone is warm, responses confirm and extend the student's reasoning (e.g., affirming the perfect-number observation), and back-and-forth feels natural. Slightly mechanical due to the rigid OPTIONS format, but actionable throughout.

effectiveness.rationale: Final code outputs 31626 correctly, equivalent to baseline. Solution quality is comparable.

friction.rationale: Three turns and ~67s vs baseline's one turn and ~21s, but the extra length delivers proportional pedagogical value through two genuine decision points and student reasoning checks.
